sentences.text: [TONE SOUNDING]
My name is Reuben Drehspul.
D-R-E-H-S-P-U-L.
At birth, my surname was D-R-E-I-S-P-U-L.
No.
18th of March, 1930.
Is 56 years old.
I made a mistake. 65 years old.
I was born in Kowno, [?
K-O-W-N-O, ?] Lithuania.
In 1940, the Russians occupied us.
A year later, in 1941, the German occupied us.
And I-- and I was 11 years old when the German occupied us.
My late mother was Zelda.
She was a-- she was a terrific mother and a good wife to my late father.
She gave her life for us.
Then my eldest sister, Shoshana.
She's here in South Africa now.
And then I had a late brother, which perished in Dachau concentration camp in Germany.
And then a little sister, Miriam.
She was liquidated by the Germans in Lithuania.
My, my late father's name was Josef.
And he was a very well to-do man.
He was an electrical contractor and employed people.
He actually used to do big works.
He actually used to work for the state president as well in Lithuania because he was a a, very high-graded electrician.
At that time, I was in beginning of schooling, and the relationship was very well.
We spoke inside Yiddish, outside was Lithuanian.
I never spoke even-- after we spoke Polish and Russian.
And I went to Hebrew school, and I learned Hebrew as well.
The home language was Yiddish.
We lived in an apartment on the main street in Kovno, which called [NON-ENGLISH], number 66.
The apartment was on the second floor.
And the, the big electrical store for my father, my late father's material in the [INAUDIBLE] was on the ground floor.
He had a very big store.
And we used to import the material from Germany before the war.
And we had a maid, and we had a very nice apartment.
And the neighborhood was excellent.
In this apartment were Jewish people and non-Jewish people, and it was situated opposite [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is like a, a garden with flowers, with trees.
People used to walk around.
And there was actually inside the garden was the Jewish theater.
I forgot about this.
And in the back of this garden was the Jewish school, the gymnasium.
And I went to the [INAUDIBLE] gymnasium, learned Hebrew.
And when the Russian comes, they didn't allow to learn Hebrew, so we learned Yiddish in public school.
And this [NON-ENGLISH] was the main street in Kovno.
It was a double carriageway, and in the middle was-- between left and right side of the road, was in the middle-- a walking, a walking place that you can walk all the way.
And there was kiosks, ice cream, ice cream vendors.
And right on top on this street was the big cathedral.
And we used to call it-- I've forgotten the name at present.
But I'll remember just now.
And it was a beautiful city.
We had trees.
We had water supplies, sanitation, health care, and so on.
And we were a well to-do family.
Schooling, unfortunately, I didn't have too much.
When the Russian came, I was still, I think, in grade one.
But we were not allowed to learn Hebrew, so we went to [INAUDIBLE].
And the Jewish people, in particular, was very well liked.
They were rich people, and they were working-class people.
Some of my relation were very rich.
Some of my relation were very poor.
But we used to help each other.
I, I remember every Saturday we used to go to a different uncles or to a different cousin to discuss matters, talking of the children with the adults.
And we used to come from [INAUDIBLE] for, for lunch.
And it's not the same people as today.
They were interested in you.
They asked you how you are.
Did you eaten?
Have you got money?
Especially when it used to come Hanukkah.
This was the most important thing for us children.
We used to go to collect Hanukkah gelt, from one uncle or auntie or cousin.
And we used to be entertained.
My late father's family were very musically inclined.
They both played guitars, and they used to sing very nicely.
The other-- my late other father's brother was a, a transport contractor.
And he was well to do, but I think he got killed on the way to Russia during the war, when the war started.
My immediate family was very well dressed.
The food at home was excellent, as far as I am concerned now.
We were nothing short of.
And the atmosphere at home was excellent.
No fighting, no shouting at the table, and we carried a Kosher home.
Every Friday night we used to go to shul.
We believed [INAUDIBLE] religious, and we were religious.
A [INAUDIBLE] is a [INAUDIBLE].
It's a, it's a certain species of Jewish people.
And what they used to say, they meant.
And we used to go to shul Friday evening.
We never drove a car, or a bus, or a bicycle.
And we used to visit mostly Saturday.
We used to take a walk and visit the family and so on.
And school friends, I actually got to one of my brothers from my late father a bicycle.
It was made in Germany.
But when the Germans occupied us, they took it away from me.

And this was-- when, when the Russian came, the Jewish people were very pleased about it because they actually thought the Germans would occupy us because of disagreement between Poland, Germany, and Russia.
But we didn't know how long the Russians would be staying in Lithuania.
A year later, the German bombed us.
And we were occupied by the Germans two weeks after the bombing.
And when the Germans bombed us, five o'clock at night, you know, in the morning, and they bombed all the cities in the Baltic States.
And it took them 10 or 12 days to occupy, to come to us.
But in the meantime, the Lithuanians start to slaughter the Jewish people.
Our neighbor downstairs, which was one of the biggest [NON-ENGLISH]-- it means against the Jewish.
[NON-ENGLISH] was an organization that didn't like the Jewish people, and didn't like the Russians.
They came to us and they say, Drehspuls, you can't stay in your flat.
You'll stay in my flat because there's going to be pogroms.
And we stayed in his flat, I think for a week or 10 days.
And were shooting.
And then came in the Germans.

But before the Germans came in, those [NON-ENGLISH], the green uniform civilian Lithuanians, which hated the Jews very much, arrested the Jewish people, shot them, and took them to the Ninth and Seventh fort.
It's a fortress place where there used to be garrisons of army and so on before.
And the Jewish people used to lie there under the sky for days.
And they used to have a machine gun that used to shoot at them.
And my late father-- and we were told not to go out of the flat, because it's dangerous.
And my late father decided to go to buy cigarettes or something like this.
And he was captured by an ex-employee of his who used to work for him.
And, and he drove him down to the fort, and he had been in the fort for two weeks, two and a half weeks.
And they used to catch people in the street, used to beat up the people.
And it was unbearable, although I didn't see with my eyes, yet.
And after two weeks-- and those Lithuanian neighbors start to look.
What happened Mr.
Drehspul?
Where is he?
Maybe one of his organization picked him up.
And they find out that he is on the Seventh or Ninth Fort.
And in the meantime, my father, while he was on the-- oh, no.
Sorry.
It's a mistake.
One day, my father decided to buy cigarettes.
And the Lithuanian wife begged him, don't go.
It's too dangerous.
You can't get cigarettes.
My husband will bring you cigarettes.
And I [INAUDIBLE] my father walked out.
And he was picked up by the Lithuanian, And they took him to the fort.
And he has been there for two and a half weeks or something like is.
They used to shoot with a machine gun at random at the people in the yard, in the big fortress yard.
And after these two weeks, he met another Lithuanian, which was actually in charge there.
And they say to him, Mr.
Drehspul, you've still got your motorbike?
He said, yes.
You know what I'll do with you?
You give me your motorbike, and you go free.
He say, fine.
And one afternoon came my late father with this Lithuanian.
And the Lithuanian took the motorbike, and they let my father free.
And we remained for another two weeks at this military men, where we stayed for this period, which was an unrest.
And there was a pogrom, especially in [INAUDIBLE], They killed the Jewish people, murdered them.
And when the Germans came in, the Lithuanians will not allow it, to kill the Jewish people or do anything to them.
There was big posters in town.
First of all, you shouldn't-- anybody who [NON-ENGLISH], means robs or rapes, or do anything to anybody will be bitterly punished.
And a few months later came out the law that the Jewish people must surrender their valuable, gold, silver, diamonds, and radio receivers or transmitters.
And all the musical instruments must be surrendered to the Germans in certain places.
Whatever we had, we had to give-- to give it in to the Germans.
And you didn't get a receipt.
I think some people even got a receipt that they surrendered their valuables.
And then came out an order that a certain date-- I don't know, I think it was in September-- that all Jewish people from Kovno must move from the main city to [NON-ENGLISH].
This was in 1942.
I think the ghetto established in '42.
I'm not-- I think-- I think so.
And you should have seen how the Jewish people are [INAUDIBLE].
Horses with waggons and cart their belongings and schlepping furniture and schlepping whatever they possessed.
And the ghetto was established in a matter of 15 or 20 days.
We actually settled in a house which belonged to my great uncle.
This was not far from a main road who connects Kovno to another city.
And it didn't take long that the German decided that it's no good to have the ghetto on the main road.
So they pushed the ghetto and made it slightly, four streets, smaller.
And in this time, while we'd been in this particular area, the Lithuanian robbed and killed people as well in the ghetto, although it was separated from the other places.
Then the Germans decided to make a judenrat.
It means a Jewish office and to be Jewish police.
And Jewish people who should see about arranging work for the Jewish people where it has to be required for certain departments.
And they start to register the people of the ghetto.
And my late father registered as an electrician, as he was a qualified electrician.
And you had to register the family, and the ages, and so on.
And from time to time, the ghetto started to become smaller.
They used to come into the ghetto, and they used to take away women, children, or whole families for other places to be worked at, like in factories, but not in Kovno itself, outside Kovno.
And they established working camps around Kovno.
My late uncle was also there.
They took him with complete family.
And they worked in certain places.
The main work, the main job in Kovno ghetto was for the people to go to work on the aircraft [INAUDIBLE], where the planes used to land.
They used to help to service them.
They used to bring bombs or other explosives that planes should take from place to place.
And there, a lot of people died.
They used to march five or six or seven kilometers every morning and back again.
Food we used to get once a week.
I think it was coupons.
I can't remember at the beginning.
I think it was coupons we could buy.
Yes, we used to buy actually.
We used to be paid with German currency at work, and then we used to buy food, which is-- it's laughable, especially in winter.
We used to get frozen potatoes.
We used to see the potato.
When you come home, you see mash.
You couldn't even cook it.
And then after a day or two, they start to smell, excuse me, terrible.
And we used to buy by the coupons bread, flour and other stuff.
The main survival in the ghetto was to organize how to sell your belongings.
You used to trade in over the fence with the Lithuanians your clothing, your-- if somebody had some money, or diamonds, or goods, outfits, bed sheets, over the wall, over-- not far from the cemetery.
And the [NON-ENGLISH], the non-Jewish, they used to give you meat, fruit, flour, potatoes, whatever they had.
And the ghetto, from time to time, became smaller.
Oh, by the way, they builded another ghetto on the other side of the road, which are-- because some people came from some other places, and the ghetto was too small.
And they built a bridge between the two ghettos.
And at the bottom of the bridge was the main road, which is going to [NON-ENGLISH] Kovno, and to the outskirts somewhere.
And in, in the main ghetto was also a [NON-ENGLISH].
But the Germans-- excuse me-- the Germans didn't show any films there.
They put accommodation for people who used to come in.
They used to sleep on the floor, people come from villages, Jewish people.
And they used to stay there.
And the people used to carry on working there.
They established a big Jewish hospital in the main ghetto, and a hospital in the small ghetto.
I frequently, I used to visit the small ghetto to visit friends or relations.
I can't remember exactly.
The atmosphere in the ghetto was very bad.
Rumors came that the Germans were burning the people in Poland.
Crematoriums had been built.
And also, they established ghettos in Latvia and Estonia.
And by the way, they took people from our ghetto, in 1942 or 43, and sent them to Estonia and Latvia, which was a very hard camps.
They worked the people very hard, and the food was bad.
Then all of a sudden, the Germans used to come in, used to surround the ghetto.
They used to come into the ghetto and take away just people at random.
And the life carried on.
We had electricity.
We had water supplies, which we didn't pay for it because the Germans stopped paying for the labor.
And, and the ghetto became smaller.
We had to move further back inside the ghetto because people has been liquidated, taken away to other places.
And the Germans decided to make the ghetto smaller.
And life carried on, very hard.
Especially in winter, there was no timber, no wood to make a fire, although every house, every flat had a fireplace.
So we decided to dismantle the fences and any furniture and make it as firewood.
And we survived 1942 and 43.
And I think in 1942, '43, there was a big action.
And they took away half of the ghetto been liquidated at the forts.
Yes.
First of all, I got a special certificate, a work certificate, stating that I, Reuben Drehspul is employed in the workshops in the ghetto under a sewing machine mechanic.

Then I had a special permit, a [NON-ENGLISH], which stated-- and this [NON-ENGLISH] only good people, tradesman, or people, professional people who were needed for the German's support, like electricians, tradesmen, doctors, or people who worked in the big workshops.
In the workshops they produced clothing.
And there was big laundries as well.
The Germans used to bring in laundry to be cleaned from the front or whatever it was.
It used to be repaired and washed.
I think my sister worked in the [INAUDIBLE] in the laundries-- I'm not sure-- outside the-- outside the ghetto.
And we used to get special facilities, maybe extra coupon to get an extra piece of bread or whatever it is.
We didn't walk around in the streets, in particular children.
We childrens, we children, were the first one to feel that there was something going to be a wrong in the ghetto.
And one morning, we woke up six o'clock.
The men went to work, and the ghetto was surrounded by the Germans and Lithuanians.
And they went from house to house and picked up women who didn't have work permit, sick people, and children.
And it was actually an action by the Germans to take out those people, in particular, the children.
And my late mother didn't work.
My late sister, my late brother, and myself.
And we hide under a staircase.
And the first two days were white Russians and Lithuanians searching for sick people, and children, and so on.
And they didn't find us.
The main [? model ?] was to, was to take away from the Jewish people the valuables, maybe even to rape girls.
I don't know.
I was a child.
I didn't seen it.
And for the [INAUDIBLE], the Germans were not happy.
So they themselves, the Wehrmacht and the Gestapo, went from house to house look for their achievement, to take away women without permits, children, and sick people.
And the Germans find us [INAUDIBLE] occupied our block.
We hide ourself under, under a staircase.
For three days the white Russians didn't find us.
Their aim was to find people as it comes, they find it.
But they wanted to amuse themselves.
And the Germans were not happy with their achievements.
So they themselves went to the ghetto to look for those victims.
